title: AI Agents: What Healthcare Leaders Need to Know
description: How autonomous AI systems are moving from pilot programs to production in health systems, and what that means for operations, staffing, and strategy.
summary:
AI agents represent a fundamental shift from tools that assist humans to systems that act independently. Unlike traditional AI that answers questions or generates text, agents can execute multi-step workflows: scheduling patient appointments, processing prior authorizations, coordinating care transitions, and even managing supply chain orders without human intervention.

The healthcare implications are massive. McKinsey projects that AI agents will handle over $1 trillion in annual transactions by 2028, with healthcare representing the fastest-growing vertical. Early adopters like Cleveland Clinic and Kaiser Permanente are already deploying agents for revenue cycle management, clinical documentation, and population health outreach. The key differentiator is that agents don't just recommend actions; they take them.

For health system leaders, the strategic question isn't whether to adopt AI agents but how to govern them. Organizations need clear frameworks for agent authority levels, escalation protocols, audit trails, and liability allocation. The systems that build this governance infrastructure now will have a significant advantage as agent capabilities accelerate through 2026 and beyond.

questions.explanation: Cleveland Clinic was cited as an early adopter deploying AI agents for revenue cycle management, clinical documentation, and population health outreach.
